<!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17225818]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Yuta-jinja 01.jpg | location = Kodoyama 983, Yuta, Obata-cho, [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.503972|N|136.649278|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Kamuo Ichihime|WD=Q11588867}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Traditionally during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = | Rituals = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | location = {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Yuta Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Inner Shrine) of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], located in [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan. It Ranks ninth among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref> The shrine enshrines deities associated with the protection of {{ill|agriculture|ja|農耕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agriculture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=farming|WD=Q24886094}}.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 65.</ref>
== Overview ==
{{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}} Omata-cho, Yuda, Aza Kozan 983<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 43</ref>, located at the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Yuda|ja|湯田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|Human settlement|en|Human settlement|WD=Q486972|lt=settlement}}<ref name="sk4">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 234</ref>. The {{ill|place name|ja|地名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place name|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=toponym|WD=Q7884789}} "Yuda" is believed to derive from "Yuta," meaning {{ill|sacred rice field|ja|斎田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred rice field|qq=}}, indicating that the area around the shrine was once sacred rice fields for Ise Jingū, surrounded by paddy fields<ref name="ic"/>. These sacred fields were developed by the {{ill|Arakida clan|ja|荒木田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakida clan|qq=}}, who served as priests of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Inner Shrine|ja|内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|qq=}}<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65</ref>.
The {{ill|shrine precinct|ja|社地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine precinct|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}} covers 463 {{ill|Pyeong|en|Pyeong|WD=Q706688|lt=tsubo}} (approximately 1,530.6 m²)<ref name="sk5">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 235</ref>. The {{ill|shrine building|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} is larger than those of other auxiliary shrines of Ise Jingū<ref name="ic"/>. The building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with {{ill|board roofing|ja|板葺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/board roofing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 杮葺|1=wood shingle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q199388}}, facing south, surrounded by a single-layer {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=tamagaki}} fence, and features one {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii}}<ref name="sk5"/>. There is no physical {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} enshrined<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59</ref>.
Each year on {{ill|December 12|en|December 12|WD=Q2369|lt=December 12}}, there is a tradition called "Auxiliary Shrine {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}}," where locals visit Yuda Shrine<ref name="sk5"/>. Other shrines under the jurisdiction of Ise Jingū that attract such secular faith include {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|ja|子安神社_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|qq=}}, {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Akasaki Shrine|ja|赤崎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaki Shrine|qq=}}<ref>Sakurai (1991): p. 294</ref>.
== Deities ==
The {{ill|Saijin|lt=Saijin|lt=Enshrined deities|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/enshrined deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} are {{ill|Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja|大歳御祖命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Mimae no Kami|ja|御前神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="ic"/>. Both are considered {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=agricultural deities}} of the local region<ref name="ic"/><ref name="tm"/>. Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto refers to {{ill|Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja|神大市比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神大市比売|1=Kamuo Ichihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588867}}, the mother of {{ill|Toshigami|ja|大歳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}<ref name="tm"/>. Some theories suggest that instead of Mimae no Kami, {{ill|Naru Ikazuchi|ja|鳴宸電|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naru Ikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is worshipped<ref name="tm"/>.
In {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=ancient times}}, there were two shrine buildings measuring 4.5 {{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|WD=Q931805|lt=shaku}} (approx. 1.36 m) long, 4 shaku (approx. 1.21 m) wide, and 3 shaku (approx. 90.9 cm) high<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref>. However, after being restored in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, one of the buildings was discontinued<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>, and the two deities now share a single shrine building<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10</ref>.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}, and in ancient times, it was one of the six shrines whose reconstruction was overseen by the {{ill|Zōjingūshi|ja|造神宮使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōjingūshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="sk4"/>. However, in the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}}, reconstruction became irregular, with materials prepared for rebuilding in {{ill|Bun'ei|en|Bun'ei|WD=Q1010103|lt=Bunei}} 4 (1267) but the work left unfinished. In later periods, reconstruction was carried out by local leaders, with the last recorded rebuilding ordered using {{ill|corvée rice|ja|役夫工米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corvée rice|1=corvée rice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488146}} in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei}} 3 (1471)<ref name="sk4"/>. After that, records of reconstruction ceased, and it seems the shrine was revered as a local {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}<ref name="sk4"/>.
On {{ill|August 28|en|August 28|WD=Q2817|lt=August 28 (Lunisolar)}}, {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun}} 3 (Gregorian calendar: September 29, 1663), the shrine was revived by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河辺精長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|1=Kiyonaga Kawabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q92924151}} (also known as Ōnakatomi Masanaga) within the precincts of a local Ujigami shrine then called "Yuda Shrine"<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 234–235</ref>. Until the local Ujigami was merged with {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine|ja|八柱神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahashira Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八王子神社}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 22 (1889), both the Ujigami and the auxiliary shrine of Ise Jingū coexisted. Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine was later merged with {{ill|Arita Shrine|ja|有田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), and its former site became a bamboo grove<ref name="sk5"/>.
The shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 12 (1923)<ref name="uy"/> and underwent major renovations in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 56 (1981)<ref name="sk5"/>.
== Access ==
The area around the shrine is rich in archaeological sites, including relics like {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}, as well as {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}, which are thought to be connected to the shrine's origin and development<ref name="sk5"/>.
* From {{ill|Iseshi Station|en|Iseshi Station|WD=Q6079748|lt=Ise-shi Station}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|WD=Q872023|lt=Sangū Line}} (operated by [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]]) and {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|WD=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu}} bus bound for {{ill|Hayamaseguchi|ja|早馬瀬口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayamaseguchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for about 15 minutes<ref name="tm"/>. Get off at the "Yuda" bus stop and walk approximately 8 minutes (about 400 m)<ref name="ic2">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 51</ref>. The shrine is about 200 m from {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 37|ja|三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 37|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11357923}}<ref name="sk4"/>. * About 14 minutes (approx. 7.0 km) by car from {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki Interchange|1=Tamaki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572465}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|en|Ise Expressway|WD=Q6079578|lt=Ise Expressway}}.
== References ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17225818]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Yuta-jinja 01.jpg | location = Kodoyama 983, Yuta, Obata-cho, [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.503972|N|136.649278|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Kamuo Ichihime|WD=Q11588867}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Traditionally during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = | Rituals = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | location = {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Yuta Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Inner Shrine) of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], located in [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan. It Ranks ninth among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref> The shrine enshrines deities associated with the protection of {{ill|agriculture|ja|農耕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agriculture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=farming|WD=Q24886094}}.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 65.</ref>
== Overview ==
{{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}} Omata-cho, Yuda, Aza Kozan 983<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 43</ref>, located at the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Yuda|ja|湯田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|Human settlement|en|Human settlement|WD=Q486972|lt=settlement}}<ref name="sk4">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 234</ref>. The {{ill|place name|ja|地名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place name|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=toponym|WD=Q7884789}} "Yuda" is believed to derive from "Yuta," meaning {{ill|sacred rice field|ja|斎田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred rice field|qq=}}, indicating that the area around the shrine was once sacred rice fields for Ise Jingū, surrounded by paddy fields<ref name="ic"/>. These sacred fields were developed by the {{ill|Arakida clan|ja|荒木田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakida clan|qq=}}, who served as priests of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Inner Shrine|ja|内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|qq=}}<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65</ref>.
The {{ill|shrine precinct|ja|社地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine precinct|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}} covers 463 {{ill|Pyeong|en|Pyeong|WD=Q706688|lt=tsubo}} (approximately 1,530.6 m²)<ref name="sk5">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 235</ref>. The {{ill|shrine building|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} is larger than those of other auxiliary shrines of Ise Jingū<ref name="ic"/>. The building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with {{ill|board roofing|ja|板葺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/board roofing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 杮葺|1=wood shingle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q199388}}, facing south, surrounded by a single-layer {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=tamagaki}} fence, and features one {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii}}<ref name="sk5"/>. There is no physical {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} enshrined<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59</ref>.
Each year on {{ill|December 12|en|December 12|WD=Q2369|lt=December 12}}, there is a tradition called "Auxiliary Shrine {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}}," where locals visit Yuda Shrine<ref name="sk5"/>. Other shrines under the jurisdiction of Ise Jingū that attract such secular faith include {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|ja|子安神社_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|qq=}}, {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Akasaki Shrine|ja|赤崎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaki Shrine|qq=}}<ref>Sakurai (1991): p. 294</ref>.
== Deities ==
The {{ill|Saijin|lt=Saijin|lt=Enshrined deities|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/enshrined deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} are {{ill|Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja|大歳御祖命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Mimae no Kami|ja|御前神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="ic"/>. Both are considered {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=agricultural deities}} of the local region<ref name="ic"/><ref name="tm"/>. Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto refers to {{ill|Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja|神大市比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神大市比売|1=Kamuo Ichihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588867}}, the mother of {{ill|Toshigami|ja|大歳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}<ref name="tm"/>. Some theories suggest that instead of Mimae no Kami, {{ill|Naru Ikazuchi|ja|鳴宸電|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naru Ikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is worshipped<ref name="tm"/>.
In {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=ancient times}}, there were two shrine buildings measuring 4.5 {{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|WD=Q931805|lt=shaku}} (approx. 1.36 m) long, 4 shaku (approx. 1.21 m) wide, and 3 shaku (approx. 90.9 cm) high<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref>. However, after being restored in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, one of the buildings was discontinued<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>, and the two deities now share a single shrine building<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10</ref>.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}, and in ancient times, it was one of the six shrines whose reconstruction was overseen by the {{ill|Zōjingūshi|ja|造神宮使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōjingūshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="sk4"/>. However, in the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}}, reconstruction became irregular, with materials prepared for rebuilding in {{ill|Bun'ei|en|Bun'ei|WD=Q1010103|lt=Bunei}} 4 (1267) but the work left unfinished. In later periods, reconstruction was carried out by local leaders, with the last recorded rebuilding ordered using {{ill|corvée rice|ja|役夫工米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corvée rice|1=corvée rice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488146}} in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei}} 3 (1471)<ref name="sk4"/>. After that, records of reconstruction ceased, and it seems the shrine was revered as a local {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}<ref name="sk4"/>.
On {{ill|August 28|en|August 28|WD=Q2817|lt=August 28 (Lunisolar)}}, {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun}} 3 (Gregorian calendar: September 29, 1663), the shrine was revived by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河辺精長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|1=Kiyonaga Kawabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q92924151}} (also known as Ōnakatomi Masanaga) within the precincts of a local Ujigami shrine then called "Yuda Shrine"<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 234–235</ref>. Until the local Ujigami was merged with {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine|ja|八柱神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahashira Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八王子神社}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 22 (1889), both the Ujigami and the auxiliary shrine of Ise Jingū coexisted. Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine was later merged with {{ill|Arita Shrine|ja|有田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), and its former site became a bamboo grove<ref name="sk5"/>.
The shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 12 (1923)<ref name="uy"/> and underwent major renovations in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 56 (1981)<ref name="sk5"/>.
== Access ==
The area around the shrine is rich in archaeological sites, including relics like {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}, as well as {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}, which are thought to be connected to the shrine's origin and development<ref name="sk5"/>.
* From {{ill|Iseshi Station|en|Iseshi Station|WD=Q6079748|lt=Ise-shi Station}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|WD=Q872023|lt=Sangū Line}} (operated by [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]]) and {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|WD=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu}} bus bound for {{ill|Hayamaseguchi|ja|早馬瀬口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayamaseguchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for about 15 minutes<ref name="tm"/>. Get off at the "Yuda" bus stop and walk approximately 8 minutes (about 400 m)<ref name="ic2">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 51</ref>. The shrine is about 200 m from {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 37|ja|三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 37|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11357923}}<ref name="sk4"/>. * About 14 minutes (approx. 7.0 km) by car from {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki Interchange|1=Tamaki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572465}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|en|Ise Expressway|WD=Q6079578|lt=Ise Expressway}}.
== References ==

ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、1983rd year （昭和58th year ）8月10日、276p. * [[櫻井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮の祖型と展開』[[国書刊行会]]、1991st year （平成3rd year ）11月30日、318p. ISBN 4-336-03296-3 * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』{{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|WD=Q1797584|lt=Kogakkan University}}出版部、1990th year （平成2nd year ）2月28日、690p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|ja|伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[有田村 (三重県)]]

== AUTOGENERATED CONTENT FROM WIKIDATA ==
'''Yuta Shrine (Q17225818)''' is a shrine in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is located in [[Ise Province]].

The {{ill|place name|ja|地名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place name|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=toponym|WD=Q7884789}} "Yuda" is believed to derive from "Yuta," meaning {{ill|sacred rice field|ja|斎田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred rice field|qq=}}, indicating that the area around the shrine was once sacred rice fields for Ise Jingū, surrounded by paddy fields<ref name="ic"/>. There is no physical {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} enshrined<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59</ref>.
Each year on {{ill|December 12|en|December 12|WD=Q2369|lt=December 12}}, there is a tradition called "Auxiliary Shrine {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}}," where locals visit Yuda Shrine<ref name="sk5"/>. Other shrines under the jurisdiction of Ise Jingū that attract such secular faith include {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|ja|子安神社_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|qq=}}, {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Akasaki Shrine|ja|赤崎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaki Shrine|qq=}}<ref>Sakurai (1991): p. 294</ref>.
== Deities ==
The {{ill|Saijin|lt=Saijin|lt=Enshrined deities|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/enshrined deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} are {{ill|Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja|大歳御祖命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Mimae no Kami|ja|御前神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="ic"/>. Both are considered {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=agricultural deities}} of the local region<ref name="ic"/><ref name="tm"/>. Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto refers to {{ill|Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja|神大市比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神大市比売|1=Kamuo Ichihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588867}}, the mother of {{ill|Toshigami|ja|大歳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}<ref name="tm"/>. Some theories suggest that instead of Mimae no Kami, {{ill|Naru Ikazuchi|ja|鳴宸電|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naru Ikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is worshipped<ref name="tm"/>.
In {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=ancient times}}, there were two shrine buildings measuring 4.5 {{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|WD=Q931805|lt=shaku}} (approx. 1.36 m) long, 4 shaku (approx. 1.21 m) wide, and 3 shaku (approx. 90.9 cm) high<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref>. However, after being restored in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, one of the buildings was discontinued<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>, and the two deities now share a single shrine building<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10</ref>.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}, and in ancient times, it was one of the six shrines whose reconstruction was overseen by the {{ill|Zōjingūshi|ja|造神宮使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōjingūshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="sk4"/>. However, in the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}}, reconstruction became irregular, with materials prepared for rebuilding in {{ill|Bun'ei|en|Bun'ei|WD=Q1010103|lt=Bunei}} 4 (1267) but the work left unfinished. In later periods, reconstruction was carried out by local leaders, with the last recorded rebuilding ordered using {{ill|corvée rice|ja|役夫工米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corvée rice|1=corvée rice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488146}} in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei}} 3 (1471)<ref name="sk4"/>. After that, records of reconstruction ceased, and it seems the shrine was revered as a local {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}<ref name="sk4"/>.
On {{ill|August 28|en|August 28|WD=Q2817|lt=August 28 (Lunisolar)}}, {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun}} 3 (Gregorian calendar: September 29, 1663), the shrine was revived by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河辺精長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|1=Kiyonaga Kawabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q92924151}} (also known as Ōnakatomi Masanaga) within the precincts of a local Ujigami shrine then called "Yuda Shrine"<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 234–235</ref>. Until the local Ujigami was merged with {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine|ja|八柱神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahashira Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八王子神社}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 22 (1889), both the Ujigami and the auxiliary shrine of Ise Jingū coexisted. Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine was later merged with {{ill|Arita Shrine|ja|有田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), and its former site became a bamboo grove<ref name="sk5"/>.
The shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 12 (1923)<ref name="uy"/> and underwent major renovations in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 56 (1981)<ref name="sk5"/>.
== Access ==
The area around the shrine is rich in archaeological sites, including relics like {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}, as well as {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}, which are thought to be connected to the shrine's origin and development<ref name="sk5"/>.
* From {{ill|Iseshi Station|en|Iseshi Station|WD=Q6079748|lt=Ise-shi Station}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|WD=Q872023|lt=Sangū Line}} (operated by [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]]) and {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|WD=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu}} bus bound for {{ill|Hayamaseguchi|ja|早馬瀬口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayamaseguchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for about 15 minutes<ref name="tm"/>. Get off at the "Yuda" bus stop and walk approximately 8 minutes (about 400 m)<ref name="ic2">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 51</ref>. The shrine is about 200 m from {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 37|ja|三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 37|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11357923}}<ref name="sk4"/>. * About 14 minutes (approx. 7.0 km) by car from {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki Interchange|1=Tamaki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572465}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|en|Ise Expressway|WD=Q6079578|lt=Ise Expressway}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17225818]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Yuta-jinja 01.jpg | location = Kodoyama 983, Yuta, Obata-cho, [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.503972|N|136.649278|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Kamuo Ichihime|WD=Q11588867}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Traditionally during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = | Rituals = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | location = {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Yuta Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Inner Shrine) of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], located in [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan. It Ranks ninth among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref> The shrine enshrines deities associated with the protection of {{ill|agriculture|ja|農耕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agriculture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=farming|WD=Q24886094}}.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 65.</ref>
== Overview ==
{{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}} Omata-cho, Yuda, Aza Kozan 983<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 43</ref>, located at the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Yuda|ja|湯田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|Human settlement|en|Human settlement|WD=Q486972|lt=settlement}}<ref name="sk4">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 234</ref>. The {{ill|place name|ja|地名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place name|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=toponym|WD=Q7884789}} "Yuda" is believed to derive from "Yuta," meaning {{ill|sacred rice field|ja|斎田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred rice field|qq=}}, indicating that the area around the shrine was once sacred rice fields for Ise Jingū, surrounded by paddy fields<ref name="ic"/>. These sacred fields were developed by the {{ill|Arakida clan|ja|荒木田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakida clan|qq=}}, who served as priests of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Inner Shrine|ja|内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|qq=}}<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65</ref>.
The {{ill|shrine precinct|ja|社地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine precinct|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}} covers 463 {{ill|Pyeong|en|Pyeong|WD=Q706688|lt=tsubo}} (approximately 1,530.6 m²)<ref name="sk5">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 235</ref>. The {{ill|shrine building|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} is larger than those of other auxiliary shrines of Ise Jingū<ref name="ic"/>. The building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with {{ill|board roofing|ja|板葺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/board roofing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 杮葺|1=wood shingle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q199388}}, facing south, surrounded by a single-layer {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=tamagaki}} fence, and features one {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii}}<ref name="sk5"/>. There is no physical {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} enshrined<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59</ref>.
Each year on {{ill|December 12|en|December 12|WD=Q2369|lt=December 12}}, there is a tradition called "Auxiliary Shrine {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}}," where locals visit Yuda Shrine<ref name="sk5"/>. Other shrines under the jurisdiction of Ise Jingū that attract such secular faith include {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|ja|子安神社_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|qq=}}, {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Akasaki Shrine|ja|赤崎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaki Shrine|qq=}}<ref>Sakurai (1991): p. 294</ref>.
== Deities ==
The {{ill|Saijin|lt=Saijin|lt=Enshrined deities|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/enshrined deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} are {{ill|Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja|大歳御祖命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Mimae no Kami|ja|御前神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="ic"/>. Both are considered {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=agricultural deities}} of the local region<ref name="ic"/><ref name="tm"/>. In {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=ancient times}}, there were two shrine buildings measuring 4.5 {{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|WD=Q931805|lt=shaku}} (approx. 1.36 m) long, 4 shaku (approx. 1.21 m) wide, and 3 shaku (approx. 90.9 cm) high<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref>. However, after being restored in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, one of the buildings was discontinued<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>, and the two deities now share a single shrine building<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10</ref>.

Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine was later merged with {{ill|Arita Shrine|ja|有田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), and its former site became a bamboo grove<ref name="sk5"/>.
The shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 12 (1923)<ref name="uy"/> and underwent major renovations in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 56 (1981)<ref name="sk5"/>.
== Access ==
The area around the shrine is rich in archaeological sites, including relics like {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}, as well as {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}, which are thought to be connected to the shrine's origin and development<ref name="sk5"/>.
* From {{ill|Iseshi Station|en|Iseshi Station|WD=Q6079748|lt=Ise-shi Station}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|WD=Q872023|lt=Sangū Line}} (operated by [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]]) and {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|WD=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu}} bus bound for {{ill|Hayamaseguchi|ja|早馬瀬口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayamaseguchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for about 15 minutes<ref name="tm"/>. Get off at the "Yuda" bus stop and walk approximately 8 minutes (about 400 m)<ref name="ic2">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 51</ref>. The shrine is about 200 m from {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 37|ja|三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 37|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11357923}}<ref name="sk4"/>. * About 14 minutes (approx. 7.0 km) by car from {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki Interchange|1=Tamaki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572465}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|en|Ise Expressway|WD=Q6079578|lt=Ise Expressway}}.
== References ==

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata--> <!--interwikis from wikidata-->
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Yuta-jinja 01.jpg | location = Kodoyama 983, Yuta, Obata-cho, [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.503972|N|136.649278|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Kamuo Ichihime|WD=Q11588867}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Traditionally during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = | Rituals = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | location = {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Yuta Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Inner Shrine) of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], located in [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan. It Ranks ninth among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref> The shrine enshrines deities associated with the protection of {{ill|agriculture|ja|農耕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agriculture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=farming|WD=Q24886094}}.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 65.</ref>
== Overview ==
{{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}} Omata-cho, Yuda, Aza Kozan 983<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 43</ref>, located at the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Yuda|ja|湯田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|Human settlement|en|Human settlement|WD=Q486972|lt=settlement}}<ref name="sk4">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 234</ref>. The {{ill|place name|ja|地名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place name|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=toponym|WD=Q7884789}} "Yuda" is believed to derive from "Yuta," meaning {{ill|sacred rice field|ja|斎田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred rice field|qq=}}, indicating that the area around the shrine was once sacred rice fields for Ise Jingū, surrounded by paddy fields<ref name="ic"/>. These sacred fields were developed by the {{ill|Arakida clan|ja|荒木田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakida clan|qq=}}, who served as priests of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Inner Shrine|ja|内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|qq=}}<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65</ref>.
The {{ill|shrine precinct|ja|社地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine precinct|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}} covers 463 {{ill|Pyeong|en|Pyeong|WD=Q706688|lt=tsubo}} (approximately 1,530.6 m²)<ref name="sk5">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 235</ref>. The {{ill|shrine building|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} is larger than those of other auxiliary shrines of Ise Jingū<ref name="ic"/>. The building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with {{ill|board roofing|ja|板葺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/board roofing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 杮葺|1=wood shingle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q199388}}, facing south, surrounded by a single-layer {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=tamagaki}} fence, and features one {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii}}<ref name="sk5"/>. There is no physical {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} enshrined<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59</ref>.
Each year on {{ill|December 12|en|December 12|WD=Q2369|lt=December 12}}, there is a tradition called "Auxiliary Shrine {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}}," where locals visit Yuda Shrine<ref name="sk5"/>. Other shrines under the jurisdiction of Ise Jingū that attract such secular faith include {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|ja|子安神社_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|qq=}}, {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Akasaki Shrine|ja|赤崎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaki Shrine|qq=}}<ref>Sakurai (1991): p. 294</ref>.
== Deities ==
The {{ill|Saijin|lt=Saijin|lt=Enshrined deities|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/enshrined deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} are {{ill|Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja|大歳御祖命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Mimae no Kami|ja|御前神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="ic"/>. Both are considered {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=agricultural deities}} of the local region<ref name="ic"/><ref name="tm"/>. Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto refers to {{ill|Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja|神大市比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神大市比売|1=Kamuo Ichihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588867}}, the mother of {{ill|Toshigami|ja|大歳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}<ref name="tm"/>. Some theories suggest that instead of Mimae no Kami, {{ill|Naru Ikazuchi|ja|鳴宸電|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naru Ikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is worshipped<ref name="tm"/>.
In {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=ancient times}}, there were two shrine buildings measuring 4.5 {{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|WD=Q931805|lt=shaku}} (approx. 1.36 m) long, 4 shaku (approx. 1.21 m) wide, and 3 shaku (approx. 90.9 cm) high<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref>. However, after being restored in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, one of the buildings was discontinued<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>, and the two deities now share a single shrine building<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10</ref>.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}, and in ancient times, it was one of the six shrines whose reconstruction was overseen by the {{ill|Zōjingūshi|ja|造神宮使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōjingūshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="sk4"/>. However, in the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}}, reconstruction became irregular, with materials prepared for rebuilding in {{ill|Bun'ei|en|Bun'ei|WD=Q1010103|lt=Bunei}} 4 (1267) but the work left unfinished. In later periods, reconstruction was carried out by local leaders, with the last recorded rebuilding ordered using {{ill|corvée rice|ja|役夫工米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corvée rice|1=corvée rice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488146}} in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei}} 3 (1471)<ref name="sk4"/>. After that, records of reconstruction ceased, and it seems the shrine was revered as a local {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}<ref name="sk4"/>.
On {{ill|August 28|en|August 28|WD=Q2817|lt=August 28 (Lunisolar)}}, {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun}} 3 (Gregorian calendar: September 29, 1663), the shrine was revived by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河辺精長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|1=Kiyonaga Kawabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q92924151}} (also known as Ōnakatomi Masanaga) within the precincts of a local Ujigami shrine then called "Yuda Shrine"<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 234–235</ref>. Until the local Ujigami was merged with {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine|ja|八柱神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahashira Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八王子神社}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 22 (1889), both the Ujigami and the auxiliary shrine of Ise Jingū coexisted. Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine was later merged with {{ill|Arita Shrine|ja|有田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), and its former site became a bamboo grove<ref name="sk5"/>.
The shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 12 (1923)<ref name="uy"/> and underwent major renovations in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 56 (1981)<ref name="sk5"/>.
== Access ==
The area around the shrine is rich in archaeological sites, including relics like {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}, as well as {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}, which are thought to be connected to the shrine's origin and development<ref name="sk5"/>.
* From {{ill|Iseshi Station|en|Iseshi Station|WD=Q6079748|lt=Ise-shi Station}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|WD=Q872023|lt=Sangū Line}} (operated by [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]]) and {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|WD=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu}} bus bound for {{ill|Hayamaseguchi|ja|早馬瀬口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayamaseguchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for about 15 minutes<ref name="tm"/>. Get off at the "Yuda" bus stop and walk approximately 8 minutes (about 400 m)<ref name="ic2">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 51</ref>. The shrine is about 200 m from {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 37|ja|三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 37|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11357923}}<ref name="sk4"/>. * About 14 minutes (approx. 7.0 km) by car from {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki Interchange|1=Tamaki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572465}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|en|Ise Expressway|WD=Q6079578|lt=Ise Expressway}}.

== AUTOGENERATED CONTENT FROM WIKIDATA ==
'''Yuta Shrine (Q17225818)''' is a shrine in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō. It is located in [[Ise Province]].

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Yuta-jinja 01.jpg | location = Kodoyama 983, Yuta, Obata-cho, [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.503972|N|136.649278|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Kamuo Ichihime|WD=Q11588867}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Traditionally during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}} | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = | Rituals = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | location = {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }}
'''{{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Yuta Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Inner Shrine) of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], located in [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan. It Ranks ninth among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref> The shrine enshrines deities associated with the protection of {{ill|agriculture|ja|農耕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agriculture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=farming|WD=Q24886094}}.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 65.</ref>
== Overview ==
{{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}} Omata-cho, Yuda, Aza Kozan 983<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 43</ref>, located at the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Yuda|ja|湯田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|Human settlement|en|Human settlement|WD=Q486972|lt=settlement}}<ref name="sk4">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 234</ref>. The {{ill|place name|ja|地名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place name|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=toponym|WD=Q7884789}} "Yuda" is believed to derive from "Yuta," meaning {{ill|sacred rice field|ja|斎田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred rice field|qq=}}, indicating that the area around the shrine was once sacred rice fields for Ise Jingū, surrounded by paddy fields<ref name="ic"/>. These sacred fields were developed by the {{ill|Arakida clan|ja|荒木田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakida clan|qq=}}, who served as priests of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Inner Shrine|ja|内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|qq=}}<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65</ref>.
The {{ill|shrine precinct|ja|社地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine precinct|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}} covers 463 {{ill|Pyeong|en|Pyeong|WD=Q706688|lt=tsubo}} (approximately 1,530.6 m²)<ref name="sk5">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 235</ref>. The {{ill|shrine building|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} is larger than those of other auxiliary shrines of Ise Jingū<ref name="ic"/>. The building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with {{ill|board roofing|ja|板葺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/board roofing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 杮葺|1=wood shingle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q199388}}, facing south, surrounded by a single-layer {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=tamagaki}} fence, and features one {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii}}<ref name="sk5"/>. There is no physical {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} enshrined<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59</ref>.
Each year on {{ill|December 12|en|December 12|WD=Q2369|lt=December 12}}, there is a tradition called "Auxiliary Shrine {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}}," where locals visit Yuda Shrine<ref name="sk5"/>. Other shrines under the jurisdiction of Ise Jingū that attract such secular faith include {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|ja|子安神社_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|qq=}}, {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Akasaki Shrine|ja|赤崎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaki Shrine|qq=}}<ref>Sakurai (1991): p. 294</ref>.
== Deities ==
The {{ill|Saijin|lt=Saijin|lt=Enshrined deities|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/enshrined deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} are {{ill|Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja|大歳御祖命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Mimae no Kami|ja|御前神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="ic"/>. Both are considered {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=agricultural deities}} of the local region<ref name="ic"/><ref name="tm"/>. Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto refers to {{ill|Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja|神大市比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神大市比売|1=Kamuo Ichihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588867}}, the mother of {{ill|Toshigami|ja|大歳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}<ref name="tm"/>. Some theories suggest that instead of Mimae no Kami, {{ill|Naru Ikazuchi|ja|鳴宸電|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naru Ikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is worshipped<ref name="tm"/>.
In {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=ancient times}}, there were two shrine buildings measuring 4.5 {{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|WD=Q931805|lt=shaku}} (approx. 1.36 m) long, 4 shaku (approx. 1.21 m) wide, and 3 shaku (approx. 90.9 cm) high<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref>. However, after being restored in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, one of the buildings was discontinued<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>, and the two deities now share a single shrine building<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10</ref>.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}, and in ancient times, it was one of the six shrines whose reconstruction was overseen by the {{ill|Zōjingūshi|ja|造神宮使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōjingūshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="sk4"/>. However, in the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}}, reconstruction became irregular, with materials prepared for rebuilding in {{ill|Bun'ei|en|Bun'ei|WD=Q1010103|lt=Bunei}} 4 (1267) but the work left unfinished. In later periods, reconstruction was carried out by local leaders, with the last recorded rebuilding ordered using {{ill|corvée rice|ja|役夫工米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corvée rice|1=corvée rice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488146}} in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei}} 3 (1471)<ref name="sk4"/>. After that, records of reconstruction ceased, and it seems the shrine was revered as a local {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}<ref name="sk4"/>.
On {{ill|August 28|en|August 28|WD=Q2817|lt=August 28 (Lunisolar)}}, {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun}} 3 (Gregorian calendar: September 29, 1663), the shrine was revived by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河辺精長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|1=Kiyonaga Kawabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q92924151}} (also known as Ōnakatomi Masanaga) within the precincts of a local Ujigami shrine then called "Yuda Shrine"<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 234–235</ref>. Until the local Ujigami was merged with {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine|ja|八柱神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahashira Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八王子神社}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 22 (1889), both the Ujigami and the auxiliary shrine of Ise Jingū coexisted. Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine was later merged with {{ill|Arita Shrine|ja|有田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), and its former site became a bamboo grove<ref name="sk5"/>.
The shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 12 (1923)<ref name="uy"/> and underwent major renovations in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 56 (1981)<ref name="sk5"/>.
== Access ==
The area around the shrine is rich in archaeological sites, including relics like {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}, as well as {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}, which are thought to be connected to the shrine's origin and development<ref name="sk5"/>.
* From {{ill|Iseshi Station|en|Iseshi Station|WD=Q6079748|lt=Ise-shi Station}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|WD=Q872023|lt=Sangū Line}} (operated by [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]]) and {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|WD=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu}} bus bound for {{ill|Hayamaseguchi|ja|早馬瀬口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayamaseguchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for about 15 minutes<ref name="tm"/>. Get off at the "Yuda" bus stop and walk approximately 8 minutes (about 400 m)<ref name="ic2">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 51</ref>. The shrine is about 200 m from {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 37|ja|三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 37|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11357923}}<ref name="sk4"/>. * About 14 minutes (approx. 7.0 km) by car from {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki Interchange|1=Tamaki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572465}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|en|Ise Expressway|WD=Q6079578|lt=Ise Expressway}}.

The {{ill|shrine precinct|ja|社地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine precinct|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}} covers 463 {{ill|Pyeong|en|Pyeong|WD=Q706688|lt=tsubo}} (approximately 1,530.6 m²)<ref name="sk5">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 235</ref>. The {{ill|shrine building|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} is larger than those of other auxiliary shrines of Ise Jingū<ref name="ic"/>. The building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with {{ill|board roofing|ja|板葺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/board roofing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 杮葺|1=wood shingle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q199388}}, facing south, surrounded by a single-layer {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=tamagaki}} fence, and features one {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii}}<ref name="sk5"/>. There is no physical {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} enshrined<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59</ref>.
Each year on {{ill|December 12|en|December 12|WD=Q2369|lt=December 12}}, there is a tradition called "Auxiliary Shrine {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}}," where locals visit Yuda Shrine<ref name="sk5"/>. Other shrines under the jurisdiction of Ise Jingū that attract such secular faith include {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|ja|子安神社_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|qq=}}, {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Akasaki Shrine|ja|赤崎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaki Shrine|qq=}}<ref>Sakurai (1991): p. 294</ref>.
== Deities ==
The {{ill|Saijin|lt=Saijin|lt=Enshrined deities|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/enshrined deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} are {{ill|Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja|大歳御祖命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Mimae no Kami|ja|御前神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="ic"/>. Both are considered {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=agricultural deities}} of the local region<ref name="ic"/><ref name="tm"/>. Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto refers to {{ill|Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja|神大市比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神大市比売|1=Kamuo Ichihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588867}}, the mother of {{ill|Toshigami|ja|大歳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}<ref name="tm"/>. Some theories suggest that instead of Mimae no Kami, {{ill|Naru Ikazuchi|ja|鳴宸電|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naru Ikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is worshipped<ref name="tm"/>.
In {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=ancient times}}, there were two shrine buildings measuring 4.5 {{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|WD=Q931805|lt=shaku}} (approx. 1.36 m) long, 4 shaku (approx. 1.21 m) wide, and 3 shaku (approx. 90.9 cm) high<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref>. However, after being restored in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, one of the buildings was discontinued<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>, and the two deities now share a single shrine building<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10</ref>.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}, and in ancient times, it was one of the six shrines whose reconstruction was overseen by the {{ill|Zōjingūshi|ja|造神宮使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōjingūshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="sk4"/>. However, in the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}}, reconstruction became irregular, with materials prepared for rebuilding in {{ill|Bun'ei|en|Bun'ei|WD=Q1010103|lt=Bunei}} 4 (1267) but the work left unfinished. In later periods, reconstruction was carried out by local leaders, with the last recorded rebuilding ordered using {{ill|corvée rice|ja|役夫工米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corvée rice|1=corvée rice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488146}} in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei}} 3 (1471)<ref name="sk4"/>. After that, records of reconstruction ceased, and it seems the shrine was revered as a local {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}<ref name="sk4"/>.
On {{ill|August 28|en|August 28|WD=Q2817|lt=August 28 (Lunisolar)}}, {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun}} 3 (Gregorian calendar: September 29, 1663), the shrine was revived by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河辺精長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|1=Kiyonaga Kawabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q92924151}} (also known as Ōnakatomi Masanaga) within the precincts of a local Ujigami shrine then called "Yuda Shrine"<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 234–235</ref>. Until the local Ujigami was merged with {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine|ja|八柱神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahashira Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八王子神社}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 22 (1889), both the Ujigami and the auxiliary shrine of Ise Jingū coexisted. Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine was later merged with {{ill|Arita Shrine|ja|有田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), and its former site became a bamboo grove<ref name="sk5"/>.
The shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 12 (1923)<ref name="uy"/> and underwent major renovations in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 56 (1981)<ref name="sk5"/>.
== Access ==
The area around the shrine is rich in archaeological sites, including relics like {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}}, as well as {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}, which are thought to be connected to the shrine's origin and development<ref name="sk5"/>.
* From {{ill|Iseshi Station|en|Iseshi Station|WD=Q6079748|lt=Ise-shi Station}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|WD=Q872023|lt=Sangū Line}} (operated by [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]]) and {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|WD=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu}} bus bound for {{ill|Hayamaseguchi|ja|早馬瀬口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayamaseguchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for about 15 minutes<ref name="tm"/>. Get off at the "Yuda" bus stop and walk approximately 8 minutes (about 400 m)<ref name="ic2">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 51</ref>. The shrine is about 200 m from {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 37|ja|三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 37|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11357923}}<ref name="sk4"/>. * About 14 minutes (approx. 7.0 km) by car from {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki Interchange|1=Tamaki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572465}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|en|Ise Expressway|WD=Q6079578|lt=Ise Expressway}}.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = {{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Yuta-jinja 01.jpg | location = Kodoyama 983, Yuta, Obata-cho, [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|34.503972|N|136.649278|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = {{ill|Kamuo Ichihime|WD=Q11588867|1=Kamuo Ichihime}} | type = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Traditionally during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = | festival = | Rituals = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | location = {{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067|1=Ise, Mie}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} }}
'''{{ill|Yuta Shrine|ja|湯田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuta Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Yuta Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Inner Shrine) of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], located in [[Ise]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan. It Ranks ninth among the 27 auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref> The shrine enshrines deities associated with the protection of {{ill|agriculture|ja|農耕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agriculture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Farming|WD=Q24886094}}.<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 65.</ref>
== Overview ==
{{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise|1=Ise, Mie}} Omata-cho, Yuda, Aza Kozan 983<ref name="tm">Tatsumi Publishing (2013): p. 43</ref>, located at the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Yuda|ja|湯田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|Human settlement|en|Human settlement|WD=Q486972|lt=settlement|1=Human settlement}}<ref name="sk4">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 234</ref>. The {{ill|place name|ja|地名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/place name|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Toponym|WD=Q7884789}} "Yuda" is believed to derive from "Yuta," meaning {{ill|sacred rice field|ja|斎田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred rice field|qq=}}, indicating that the area around the shrine was once sacred rice fields for Ise Jingū, surrounded by paddy fields<ref name="ic"/>. These sacred fields were developed by the {{ill|Arakida clan|ja|荒木田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arakida clan|qq=}}, who served as priests of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Inner Shrine|ja|内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|qq=}}<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65</ref>.
The {{ill|shrine precinct|ja|社地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine precinct|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=Jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}} covers 463 {{ill|Pyeong|en|Pyeong|WD=Q706688|lt=tsubo|1=Pyeong}} (approximately 1,530.6 m²)<ref name="sk5">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 235</ref>. The {{ill|shrine building|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}} is larger than those of other auxiliary shrines of Ise Jingū<ref name="ic"/>. The building is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with {{ill|board roofing|ja|板葺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/board roofing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 杮葺|1=Wood shingle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q199388}}, facing south, surrounded by a single-layer {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=tamagaki|1=Sacred fences}} fence, and features one {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii|1=Torii}}<ref name="sk5"/>. There is no physical {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai|1=Shintai}} enshrined<ref>Kaneko (1983): p. 59</ref>.
Each year on {{ill|December 12|en|December 12|WD=Q2369|lt=December 12|1=December 12}}, there is a tradition called "Auxiliary Shrine {{ill|Kōshin-machi|ja|庚申待|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-machi|qq=}}," where locals visit Yuda Shrine<ref name="sk5"/>. Other shrines under the jurisdiction of Ise Jingū that attract such secular faith include {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|ja|子安神社_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|qq=}}, {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Katada Shrine|ja|堅田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katada Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Akasaki Shrine|ja|赤崎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaki Shrine|qq=}}<ref>Sakurai (1991): p. 294</ref>.
== Deities ==
The {{ill|Saijin|lt=Saijin|lt=Enshrined deities|ja|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/enshrined deities|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミ}} are {{ill|Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja|大歳御祖命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Mimae no Kami|ja|御前神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="ic"/>. Both are considered {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=agricultural deities|1=Deity}} of the local region<ref name="ic"/><ref name="tm"/>. Ōtoshi no Mioya no Mikoto refers to {{ill|Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja|神大市比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Ōichi-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神大市比売|1=Kamuo Ichihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588867}}, the mother of {{ill|Toshigami|ja|大歳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|qq=}}<ref name="tm"/>. Some theories suggest that instead of Mimae no Kami, {{ill|Naru Ikazuchi|ja|鳴宸電|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naru Ikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is worshipped<ref name="tm"/>.
In {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=ancient times|1=Ancient history}}, there were two shrine buildings measuring 4.5 {{ill|Shaku (unit)|en|Shaku (unit)|WD=Q931805|lt=shaku|1=Shaku (unit)}} (approx. 1.36 m) long, 4 shaku (approx. 1.21 m) wide, and 3 shaku (approx. 90.9 cm) high<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref>. However, after being restored in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, one of the buildings was discontinued<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>, and the two deities now share a single shrine building<ref name="uy">Ujiyamada City Office (1929): p. 10</ref>.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}, and in ancient times, it was one of the six shrines whose reconstruction was overseen by the {{ill|Zōjingūshi|ja|造神宮使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōjingūshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="sk4"/>. However, in the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period|1=Middle Ages}}, reconstruction became irregular, with materials prepared for rebuilding in {{ill|Bun'ei|en|Bun'ei|WD=Q1010103|lt=Bunei|1=Bun'ei}} 4 (1267) but the work left unfinished. In later periods, reconstruction was carried out by local leaders, with the last recorded rebuilding ordered using {{ill|corvée rice|ja|役夫工米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corvée rice|1=Q11488146|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488146}} in {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|WD=Q8432|lt=Bunmei|1=Civilization}} 3 (1471)<ref name="sk4"/>. After that, records of reconstruction ceased, and it seems the shrine was revered as a local {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami|1=Ubusunagami}}<ref name="sk4"/>.
On {{ill|August 28|en|August 28|WD=Q2817|lt=August 28 (Lunisolar)|1=August 28}}, {{ill|Kanbun (era)|en|Kanbun (era)|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun (era)}} 3 (Gregorian calendar: September 29, 1663), the shrine was revived by {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河辺精長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|1=Kiyonaga Kawabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q92924151}} (also known as Ōnakatomi Masanaga) within the precincts of a local Ujigami shrine then called "Yuda Shrine"<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 234–235</ref>. Until the local Ujigami was merged with {{ill|Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine|ja|八柱神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahashira Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八王子神社}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 22 (1889), both the Ujigami and the auxiliary shrine of Ise Jingū coexisted. Yahashira Shrine|lt=Yahashira Shrine|1=Hachioji Shrine was later merged with {{ill|Arita Shrine|ja|有田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arita Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 43 (1910), and its former site became a bamboo grove<ref name="sk5"/>.
The shrine was rebuilt in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} 12 (1923)<ref name="uy"/> and underwent major renovations in {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 56 (1981)<ref name="sk5"/>.
== Access ==
The area around the shrine is rich in archaeological sites, including relics like {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}}, as well as {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}}, which are thought to be connected to the shrine's origin and development<ref name="sk5"/>.
* From {{ill|Iseshi Station|en|Iseshi Station|WD=Q6079748|lt=Ise-shi Station|1=Iseshi Station}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|WD=Q872023|lt=Sangū Line|1=Sangū Line}} (operated by [[:en:JR Central|JR Central]]) and {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|WD=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line|1=Yamada Line (Kintetsu)}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|en|Mie Kotsu|WD=Q6843516|lt=Mie Kotsu|1=Mie Kotsu}} bus bound for {{ill|Hayamaseguchi|ja|早馬瀬口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayamaseguchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for about 15 minutes<ref name="tm"/>. Get off at the "Yuda" bus stop and walk approximately 8 minutes (about 400 m)<ref name="ic2">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 51</ref>. The shrine is about 200 m from {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 37|ja|三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 37|1=Mie Prefectural Road Route 37|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11357923}}<ref name="sk4"/>. * About 14 minutes (approx. 7.0 km) by car from {{ill|Tamaki Interchange|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki Interchange|1=Tamaki interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572465}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|en|Ise Expressway|WD=Q6079578|lt=Ise Expressway|1=Ise Expressway}}.
